title: The Macro Minute: Are markets too sanguine regarding the strategic decoupling of the US and China?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Delta, to
present our macro minute for Monday,
June 2nd, 2025. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 Macro.
So, today's key macro question is, are
markets too sanguine regarding the
strategic decoupling of the US and
China?
Our answer?
The recent mildly risk-off tone
continues today as China and the US
accuse each other of breaching a
weeks-old trade deal, reigniting tariff
tensions over the weekend.
While Trump declined to provide
specifics on Friday, US Trade
Representative Jameson Greer
subsequently flagged China's failure to
expedite critical mineral exports for
high-tech electronics.
China's Ministry of Commerce slammed
Trump's claims as false and said his
push for a call with Xi this week uh was
in jeopardy.
Beijing also blasted New York new US
curbs on AI chip exports, chip design
software, and Chinese student visas,
saying the US broke the January 17th
Trump-Xi consensus and vowed resolute
and forceful measures if the US
violations persist.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said
the trade spat will be ironed out in an
imminent Trump-Xi call. Recall that
Trump signaled a desire to speak with Xi
as early as February and said he's
opening to traveling to China for talks.
We view Trump's willingness to play ball
as supportive of our April 23rd pivot
from bearish paradigm B to bullish
paradigm C.
Elsewhere, Treasury Secretary Scott
Bessent provided incremental
confirmation of our paradigm C thesis
yesterday by signaling, "We
are not We are going to bring the
deficit down slowly {dot} {dot} {dot}
over the next four years." And that was
uh I want to say it was on Face the
Nation or Meet the Press on Sat- on
Sunday. Uh slowly is not quickly.
Moreover, slowly is supportive of our
view that the Trump administration aims
to deleverage the US public sector
balance sheet via reflating economic
growth as opposed to legitimate fiscal
austerity.
Long live paradigm C.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard as
always to wrap up with a question from
our community. This one's titled the
immortal top part of the K. Hello
Darius. Hope you and your family had a
great weekend. We did. Likewise my
friend. Says on around the horn you said
that as long as the top part of the K
stays intact the US economy is likely to
stay resilient. If so, is there anything
really left that could breach its armor?
Top part of the K owns the AI and robots
gradually removing the need for human
labor and further reducing the
bargaining power of the working class.
The top part is also has the financial
know-how and acts or access to it to
protect itself from market downturns and
actually benefit from them. Wars, no
problem. Someone else needs puts the
bill. Famine, not for us. Civil unrest,
meet my automated swarm of armed drones.
It has built itself into a fortress of
capability, foresight, political power,
technology, and money. Does this mean we
are now forever resilient? Um
No, not necessarily.
No, so I agree with
most of what was communicated in terms
of
how resilient the top part of the K has
made itself for a variety of the reasons
laid out in the question. I don't think
we're perpetually resilient however.
The economy would become a very
unresilient if you take away the one
thing that the top part of the K cares
about most and it's their asset prices.
You know, ask the top part of the K at
least here in the US which I would
imagine is probably just as bad if not
Sorry. I would imagine the rest of the
world is just as bad as us if not worse
just based on you know the structure of
their economies. But the top quintile
asset owners in the United States of
America by household own 90% of
financial assets.
So I mean again
that is the K-shaped US economy in a
nutshell specifically as relates to
investor exposure to financial markets.
And so
you know, if we had a significant and
sustained bear market that would
eventually cause the K the top part of
the K, to pull back enough on their
consumer spending and their investment
activity in order to allow the US
economy to grow go into recession.
You know, so that would require again a
durable bear market that ultimately
causes growth to slow faster and
ultimately wind up in recession. Now
again, unless you can
based on how the economy is shaped from
a sectoral standpoint, from a
distributional standpoint, it's very
unlikely that you wind up in an actual
recession for the those reasons and
other reasons until you had at the at
the bare minimum a preceding durable
bear market that eventually causes them
to pull back on consumption and
investment. So, I generally agree with
the sentiment of the question that the K
is incredibly resilient for all the
reasons we've been pounding the table on
for the top part of the K rather is
incredibly resilient for all the reasons
we've been pounding the table on since
we authored our since we authored the
resilient US economy theme back in
December of 2022. No change to that
view. I think we I think we've been
right in rejecting Wall Street's, you
know, going back to early April. Every
sell-side bank in the world was telling
you about was talking about recession
again and we were like absolutely not.
We're not going into recession. Just be
aware that hey, growth is probably going
to slow. We're in this U-shaped economy,
but ultimately the other side of the U
is a
recovery. And I think investors need to
be aware that the markets are very
clearly positioning for that recovery
because they see a the growth slowdown
as being transitory. Now, if the growth
slowdown was indefinite because there
was not a
a series of credible policies being
tacked onto tariffs that could
potentially resplate a US growth, then
that would be a much bigger issue for
asset markets, but clearly it's not. The
transitory slowdown in growth is not an
issue for asset markets because you have
a litany of other
pro-growth, you know, shocks
from a policy standpoint that are
currently being legislated and are
coming down the pipe from a with respect
to deregulation, etc. So, we're wrapping
up there. Barry Sternlicht presenting
our macro minute for Monday, June 2nd,
2025. Best of luck out there today.
We'll catch back here. Have a great
week.
